text:
Athletes across 48 events in the upcoming Paris Olympic Games will have an extra incentive to win a gold medal, with a five-figure cheque thrown in as another reward for their efforts.

A world sporting body has announced it is set to reward athletes who win a gold medal with a cash prize at the Paris Olympic Games in just over three months' time.

World Athletics revealed overnight its plans to pay $50,000 to competitors in its 48 events on the track and field program at the 17-day global event.

Athletes participating in events such as sprints, marathon, race walks, high jump, javelin, shot put and the decathlon, will take a share of the $3.6 million prize pool.

It has been reported the International Olympic Committee were blindsided by the move, with World Athletics the first to break a 128-year tradition to reward competitors, as it sees competing and potentially being honoured with a medal as the reward.

Its President Sebastian Coe stressed the importance of giving back to athletes who help generate revenue and make their event and the Olympics a "global spectacle".

He did not believe the move would violate the Olympic spirit.

"My view is the world has changed. It’s really important that where possible we create a sport that is financially viable for our competitors. This is the beginning of that," he said.

"If I thought athletes were only competing because there was a financial pot at the end of the day, then I might take a very different view – but they are not."

The prize money will come from the share of Olympic revenue that the IOC hands out to World Athletics and other bodies of sports.

World Athletics also vowed to also offer prize money for silver and bronze placements at the next Olympics in Los Angeles, but no exact figure was mentioned.

The cash incentive will likely be supported by most, if not all athletes.

Competitors in many countries struggle to make a living and spend a lot of money and their time dedicated to training for years leading up to the Olympics.

According to a 2020 survey from the Australian Sports Foundation, half of the athletes who competed on a national or international level earned less than $23,000 a year.

The Australian Olympic Committee has a scheme already in place that pays athletes $20,000 for gold, $15,000 for silver and $10,000 for bronze.

The only catch is they only earn one payment for their best result and on the condition they train for the next Games.

Other countries such as the US offer $37,500 for gold. Singapore offered $1 million if a competitor could bring glory to the country. Only one athlete has managed that feat.

It comes amid a plan from the "Enhanced Games" - an event similar to the Olympics where drugs can be taken - to offer major cash prizes to athletes.

Melbourne-born businessman Aron D'Souza is the Australian behind the bold move, which will see athletes compete in the five events of track and field, swimming, weightlifting, gymnastics and combat sports.

Two-time Olympic swimmerJames Magnussen has been confirmed as one of the first athletes to competeand has been offered $1.5 million if he can beat the 50 metre freestyle world record from 2009, held by Brazil's Cesar Cielo Fihlo at 20.91 seconds.
